meta.name: Department of Families, Seniors, Disability Services and Child Safety & Pensak

one.zh: 海牙公约:子女惯常居所在日本、不法滞留澳洲且无例外成立,法院命令两名(原住民)子女返回日本由当地法院裁断未来。
one.en: Hague Convention: with the children habitually resident in Japan, wrongfully retained in Australia and no exception made out, the court ordered the two (Indigenous) children returned to Japan for its courts to decide their future.

issue.zh: 两名子女被滞留澳洲前的惯常居所是否在日本?父亲主张的原住民文化联系等是否构成拒绝返还的「重大风险/无法忍受处境」例外?
issue.en: Were the children habitually resident in Japan before being retained in Australia, and do the father's claims (Aboriginal cultural connection and others) make out the 'grave risk / intolerable situation' exception to return?

subseq.zh: 返还命令不等于最佳利益终判;子女返日后,有关其照料与未来的实体问题由日本法院裁断。
subseq.en: A return order is not a final best-interests decision; once back in Japan, the substantive questions about the children's care and future fall to the Japanese courts.

summary.catchwords: CHILD ABDUCTION – Hague Convention – Application for return of children to Japan – Determination of habitual residence – Children residing in Japan for extended period – Ambiguous parental intention – Integration into Japanese family, educational and social environment – Habitual residence established in Japan – No regulatory exceptions made out – Return order made.

hint.en: The whole case as a story

html.zh:
一宗海牙公约下的子女返还案。申请人是昆士兰州一家庭与儿童安全厅(作为中央机关),代表在日本的母亲请求把两名子女送回日本;父亲是被申请人。

父亲是澳大利亚原住民男子(获 B 族与 D 族承认),两名子女 X(2018 年生)、Y(2021 年生)亦为原住民;父亲从事「飞进飞出」(FIFO)工作,不工作时与其母同住。母亲是日本人,现与子女在澳大利亚,住在与家暴支援服务相连的庇护住所,地址不公开;子女每周两次与父亲电子通讯。

法院认定:两名子女在 2024 年 9 月 4 日被不法滞留于澳大利亚之前,其惯常居所在日本;申请在不法滞留后一年内提出;父亲主张的各项例外(切断与原住民「Country」/亲族/文化的联系、剥夺与父亲的关系、母亲施暴、整体构成「无法忍受的处境」)均不成立。法院遂依规例 reg 16(1) 命令子女返回日本。
html.en:
A Hague Convention child-return case. The applicant is a Queensland department (as Central Authority) acting for the mother in Japan, seeking the return of two children to Japan; the father is the respondent.

The father is an Aboriginal man (recognised by the B and D peoples); the two children, X (2018) and Y (2021), are also Indigenous. He is a fly-in/fly-out worker, living with his mother when not working. The mother is Japanese and is now in Australia with the children, in supported accommodation connected to a family-violence service (location undisclosed); the children speak with the father twice a week electronically.

The court found the children were habitually resident in Japan immediately before their wrongful retention in Australia on 4 September 2024; the application was filed within one year; and none of the father's claimed exceptions (severance from Country/kinship/Aboriginal culture, loss of relationship with him, the mother's alleged abuse, and an overall 'intolerable situation') was made out. The court therefore ordered the children's return to Japan under reg 16(1).

html.zh:
性质:海牙《国际诱拐儿童民事方面公约》项下的返还申请,须从速处理。这类裁定不是对子女最佳利益的全面判断——它只决定由哪国法院(此处为惯常居所国日本)来裁断子女的未来。

程序:申请于 2025 年 4 月 10 日提出。开庭时父亲申请押后(理由包括来不及找日本法专家、对方誓章迟交、传票被拒等),法官逐一驳回——父亲早已掌握对方专家证据数月却未自行举证。随后法院依次处理:管辖事实(惯常居所)→ 例外抗辩 → 返还命令。母亲获授权单独办理替代旅行证件,以便子女尽快返日。
html.en:
Nature: a return application under the Hague Convention on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction, to be dealt with expeditiously. Such a decision is not a general best-interests determination — it only decides which country's courts (here Japan, the place of habitual residence) will decide the children's future.

Procedure: the application was filed on 10 April 2025. At the hearing the father sought an adjournment (grounds included no time to find a Japanese-law expert, the late filing of affidavits, and refused subpoenas), each rejected — he had held the other side's expert evidence for months and had not obtained his own. The court then worked through: jurisdictional facts (habitual residence) → the exceptions → the return order. The mother was authorised to obtain replacement travel documents alone so the children could return promptly.

html.zh:
§ reg16 ›
§ reg16(3)(b) ›
§ s111B ›
§ s140 ›
框架
返还机制在《1986 年家庭法(诱拐儿童公约)规例》:reg 16(1) 规定满足管辖事实即必须命令返还,除非成立 reg 16(3) 的例外;reg 16(3)(b) 即「重大风险/无法忍受处境」例外,门槛极高。《家庭法》s 111B 赋予公约在澳国内法上的效力;《1995 年证据法》s 140 定民事举证标准(盖然性权衡)。
html.en:
§ reg16 ›
§ reg16(3)(b) ›
§ s111B ›
§ s140 ›
Framework
The return machinery is in the Family Law (Child Abduction Convention) Regulations 1986: reg 16(1) makes a return order mandatory once the jurisdictional facts are met, unless a reg 16(3) exception is established; reg 16(3)(b) is the 'grave risk / intolerable situation' exception, with a very high threshold. Section 111B of the Family Law Act gives the Convention domestic effect; s 140 of the Evidence Act 1995 sets the civil standard (balance of probabilities).

html.zh:
法院引用高等法院与家事法院判例确立的原则:

LK v Director-General (2009) 237 CLR 582
惯常居所是事实问题,看子女实际生活的稳定融入,父母意图仅为因素之一、可含糊不定。
DP v Commonwealth Central Authority [2001] HCA 39
reg 16(3)(b)「重大风险」例外须从严解释、门槛很高,且优先考虑能否以条件化解风险。
De L v Director-General (1996) 187 CLR 640
子女异议例外的处理框架。
html.en:
The court applied principles from High Court and Family Court authority:

LK v Director-General (2009) 237 CLR 582
Habitual residence is a question of fact turning on the child's settled integration; parental intention is only one factor and may be ambiguous.
DP v Commonwealth Central Authority [2001] HCA 39
The reg 16(3)(b) 'grave risk' exception is construed narrowly with a high threshold, and the court first asks whether conditions can meet the risk.
De L v Director-General (1996) 187 CLR 640
The framework for the child's-objection exception.

html.zh:
1. 惯常居所在日本 —— 子女曾长期在日本生活,融入当地的家庭、教育与社会环境;尽管父母对长远去留意图含糊,客观上子女的惯常居所已在日本确立。2024 年 9 月 4 日起在澳被不法滞留。

2. 管辖事实齐备 —— 申请在不法滞留后一年内提出,中央机关已证明全部管辖要件,举证按民事标准(s 140)。

3. 例外均不成立 —— 父亲以原住民文化联系、与子女关系、母亲施暴主张「重大风险/无法忍受处境」;法院认为这些风险未达 reg 16(3)(b) 的极高门槛,且文化联系等可在返还后另作安排;reg 16(3)(d)(违反基本人权)亦远未触及。

4. 必须返还 —— 既然管辖事实成立而无例外,reg 16(1) 下法院有义务命令返还。返还并非对最佳利益的终局判断,而是把子女福祉问题交还惯常居所国(日本)的法院——这正是公约的前提。
html.en:
1. Habitual residence in Japan — the children had lived in Japan for an extended period and were integrated into its family, educational and social environment; although the parents' long-term intentions were ambiguous, the children's habitual residence was objectively established in Japan. They were wrongfully retained in Australia from 4 September 2024.

2. Jurisdictional facts satisfied — the application was filed within one year of the wrongful retention, and the Central Authority proved every jurisdictional element on the civil standard (s 140).

3. No exception made out — the father invoked Aboriginal cultural connection, his relationship with the children, and the mother's alleged abuse to assert a 'grave risk / intolerable situation'; the court held these did not meet the very high reg 16(3)(b) threshold and that matters such as cultural connection could be addressed after return; the reg 16(3)(d) (fundamental human rights) exception came nowhere near being engaged.

4. Return is mandatory — with the jurisdictional facts established and no exception, reg 16(1) obliges the court to order return. A return order is not a final best-interests determination; it returns questions of the children's welfare to the courts of the country of habitual residence (Japan) — the Convention's premise.

html.zh:
裁判要旨

海牙公约返还程序只解决「由哪国法院裁断子女未来」,一旦惯常居所等管辖事实成立,法院即有义务命令返还,除非成立从严解释、门槛极高的例外。

惯常居所是客观事实问题,父母意图含糊不影响其成立;原住民文化联系、与一方家长的关系等,通常不足以单独构成 reg 16(3)(b) 的「重大风险/无法忍受处境」,因这些可在返还后于惯常居所国处理。
html.en:
Ratio

Hague return proceedings decide only 'which country's courts will determine the children's future'; once the jurisdictional facts (including habitual residence) are established, the court is obliged to order return unless a narrowly construed, high-threshold exception is made out.

Habitual residence is an objective question of fact unaffected by ambiguous parental intention; Aboriginal cultural connection or a relationship with one parent will not usually, on their own, establish the reg 16(3)(b) 'grave risk / intolerable situation', as such matters can be dealt with after return in the country of habitual residence.

html.zh:
Hague Convention · 海牙公约
《1980 年国际诱拐儿童民事方面公约》,旨在迅速将被不法带走/滞留的子女送回惯常居所国。
Habitual residence · 惯常居所
子女实际稳定生活、融入的地方;是事实判断,为公约的管辖基石。
Wrongful retention · 不法滞留
在他国逾期不归、违反对方监护权;与「不法带走」并列。
reg 16(3)(b) grave risk · 重大风险例外
返还会令子女面临身心重大风险或陷入无法忍受处境的例外;门槛极高、从严解释。
Central Authority · 中央机关
各缔约国指定、负责处理公约申请的机构(本案为州厅)。
Country · 原住民「Country」
澳大利亚原住民与特定土地、亲族与文化的归属联系。
html.en:
Hague Convention
The 1980 Convention on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction — to return wrongfully removed/retained children promptly to their country of habitual residence.
Habitual residence
Where the child actually, settledly lives and is integrated; a factual question and the Convention's jurisdictional cornerstone.
Wrongful retention
Keeping a child in another country beyond agreed time, in breach of rights of custody; the counterpart to wrongful removal.
reg 16(3)(b) grave risk
The exception where return would expose the child to grave physical/psychological risk or an intolerable situation; a very high, narrowly construed threshold.
Central Authority
The body each Contracting State designates to handle Convention applications (here a State department).
Country (Aboriginal)
An Aboriginal person's belonging to particular land, kinship and culture.

html.zh:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

JUSTICE CARTER

INTRODUCTION

This is an application by the Director-General, Department of Families, Seniors, Disability Services and Child Safety (“the applicant”) filed on 10 April 2025 seeking the return of the children X also known by the mother’s last name born in 2018 and Y also known by the mother’s last name born in 2021 (“the children”) to the country of Japan pursuant to the provisions of the Family Law (Child Abduction Convention) Regulations 1986 (Cth) (“the Regulations”).

The respondent is the father of the children. The requesting parent is the mother. She is currently present in Australia with the children.

The father is an Indigenous man, recognised by the B people of C Region and the D people of E Region. The children are also Indigenous.

Since at least August 2023, the father has worked as a fly-in/fly-out worker. He lives with his mother in City F when he is not working.

The mother is Japanese. She and the children are in Australia. They are living in supported accommodation connected to a family violence support service. Their location is not disclosed.

The children currently speak with their father twice a week by electronic communication.

Adjournment application

At the commencement of the proceedings, the father sought an adjournment. The application was pressed on a number of grounds, none of which I accepted for reasons which follow. I did not permit the father to file an additional affidavit of some 44 pages on the morning of the hearing, purportedly outlining evidence in support of an adjournment application. Rather, I asked Mr G – the father’s then solicitor – to argue each ground on which he sought the matter be adjourned. The submissions lacked precision and were wide‑ranging, and I have addressed them as clearly as possible.

Mr G asserted he had not had time to adduce evidence from an expert in Japanese family law to rebut the evidence led by the applicant. The applicant filed an affidavit of Dr Nakamura on 11 August 2025 and a further affidavit on 24 March 2026. That second affidavit was late filed, and I did not permit the applicant to rely on it. The father has had the evidence of Dr Nakamura since 11 August 2025 and accordingly has had many months to find his own expert, and he has not done so. Accordingly, that does not justify an adjournment.

Mr G complained that the affidavit of Ms H was filed out of time and the applicant ought not be permitted to rely upon it or the matter needed to be adjourned as Mr G had not had sufficient time to take instructions on that document and “rebut” the mother’s evidence. The affidavit was due to be filed on 13 March 2026. It was filed four days later. That affidavit attached an affidavit of the mother previously filed on 8 August 2025 and an affidavit of the mother affirmed on 16 March 2026. There was no prejudice to the father in relation to the affidavit filed on 8 August 2025 as he had full knowledge of the contents of that affidavit for 7 months. The affidavit of 16 March 2026 was approximately 4 pages long, and was in response to the father’s trial affidavit. Again, there was no prejudice to the father in the short delay in the filing of that affidavit and I made it clear the father would be permitted to give evidence in chief if there were matters raised in that affidavit of 16 March 2026 which he had not already deposed to in his trial affidavit. Accordingly, this did not provide an appropriate basis for an adjournment.

The applicant had also sought to rely on a further affidavit of the mother filed on 24 March 2026. I did not permit the applicant to rely on that affidavit as it was filed 11 days out of time and less than a week prior to the final hearing commencing. Six paragraphs of that affidavit were subsequently tendered into evidence in re-examination as those paragraphs clarified the mother’s English language capacity, cultural differences in use of language and the shortcomings arising from using a translation application, all being matters upon which she had been cross examined. This approach was determined to be an efficient use of court time, given the mother’s language difficulties. The father was provided with the opportunity to respond to that evidence in his evidence in chief.

Mr G then asserted the Independent Children's Lawyer had only just provided a lengthy tender bundle and he needed to get instructions on that. I note that the first 140 pages of the bundle were documents filed by the father in the parenting proceedings and accordingly could not have been a surprise to the father. The balance of the documents substantially comprised correspondence to which the father was a party. Again, those documents were not new to the father. At any rate, if and when any of the documents from the bundle were sought to be tendered, objection could be made at that point. Accordingly, that issue did not provide an appropriate basis for an adjournment.

Mr G then complained that a number of subpoenas he had sought to be issued had been refused. He said it would be a miscarriage of justice if the matter proceeded without the production of the documents sought in the rejected subpoenas.

The first subpoena was to the Department of Home Affairs Legal Services. That subpoena sought the production of travel documents presumably evidencing the time the parents and children were in Australia and the times they were outside Australia. Leave had been granted on 10 February 2026 for that subpoena to be issued. It appears the subpoena was incorrectly attached to the parenting proceedings rather than these proceedings. However, Mr G informed the Court he recalled he had directed a clerk to attend to service of same. It appears however the subpoena was never served and accordingly documents were not produced. The father’s failure to attend to service does not provide an appropriate basis for an adjournment particularly where there is no controversy in this matter as to the dates the various members of the family were present in Australia or elsewhere. There was no submission made as to why the documents may otherwise have been required. Accordingly, the absence of the documents presents no prejudice to the father.

The other subpoenas were to the Department of Foreign Affairs and the Attorney-General’s Department. Leave to issue those subpoenas was refused as they were too broad, not relevant to the issues in dispute and were not in accordance with the appropriate forms. However, Mr G asserted the matter needed to be adjourned, and the subpoena issued, as the documents sought were “vital” to the father’s case as the contents were relevant to the consideration of “grave risk”.

As best as I could understand there were substantially three types of documents sought. The first class of documents sought were those exchanged between the Central Authorities regarding the application, internal documents regarding the decision to accept and proceed with the application and other documents regarding a Mr J. It appeared the father had some suspicion that the application had not emanated from the appropriate authority in Japan, but had been improperly brought by Mr J, or that there was some other defect or deficiency in the initiating processes or the decision to issue proceedings. He said if that was the case, the application ought not have been accepted by the Commonwealth Central Authority or ought not to have been issued.

No factual or evidentiary basis was identified to suggest any irregularity in the receipt, assessment or transmission of the application by the relevant Central Authorities. In the absence of any material raising a legitimate forensic issue as to authenticity or process, I was not satisfied that the documents sought were relevant to the determination of any matter properly in issue.

Secondly, the father sought access to unspecified communications or reports said to reflect concerns about the operation of the Convention on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction (“the Convention”) and domestic Japanese family law. No document was identified with sufficient particularity, nor was any cogent evidentiary basis advanced to demonstrate that the existence or content of such material could bear upon the jurisdictional requirements of reg 16(1A) of the Regulations, or the regulatory exceptions relied upon. In those circumstances, the proposed subpoenas were impermissibly broad and lacked a sufficient nexus to the issues requiring determination and accordingly did not justify adjournment of a Convention matter required to be dealt with expeditiously.

The subpoena addressed to the Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade also appeared to impose an onerous task on the department, and in some parts required the recipient to form a conclusion or make a judgment about whether a document fell within the very broad scope of the proposed subpoena.

Additionally, and as already observed, the father had ample time to adduce evidence from his own expert as to the operation of Japanese family law and had not done so. I note further the applicant had adduced evidence from a Japanese family lawyer and she was available to be cross examined by the father.

The father submitted that the applicant, as a model litigant, should disclose any material of the kind described. Counsel for the applicant advised that no such material was held or relied upon. In the absence of evidence that such documents exist, or that they were relevant to the issues in dispute, I was not satisfied that any obligation of disclosure arose which could justify an adjournment.

Thirdly the father sought the production of the original lease agreement dated November 2024. That lease was exhibited – in English – to the mother’s affidavits dated 6 December 2024 and 17 July 2025. It was apparently created by a real estate agent in Japan who asserted that the mother and father had inspected a unit in August 2024 but did not sign a lease for the property they inspected. The estate agent said a lease for another property was signed in November 2024. The estate agent asserted “they” signed that lease, which the father said was incorrect as he was not in Japan at that time. The evidence of the mother and maternal grandmother was that the maternal grandmother signed that lease. At any rate, the applicant did not rely on the lease or the letter from the estate agent.

Mr G asserted the original lease agreement would contain “meta data” that would assist in determining whether the lease was a sham. It seemed Mr G also believed that the existence of the lease signed in November 2024 had been fundamental to the determination by the applicant in accepting this application be issued. There was no rational basis for that assertion. The lease and letter were not relied on by the applicant, and I am not satisfied that the provision of the original lease document was necessary to resolve any fact in issue.

Rather, adjourning the matter so that document could be produced would have diverted the proceedings from the efficient determination of a Convention application, contrary to the objects of reg 15(2). That is particularly so given that the father conceded he did inspect a potential rental property with the mother in Japan in August 2024.

Similarly, I did not permit the father to cross examine Ms K about the lease. Ms K is the case manager at the Australian Central Authority. Her view as to the validity or otherwise of a lease document that is not relied upon has no bearing on the issues in dispute. There was no rational basis for Mr G’s assertion that the Australian Central Authority would not have accepted the application but for the existence of the lease and it was not relevant to a fact in issue.

None of the arguments regarding subpoena or the refusal to grant leave to issue the subpoena, nor any other argument advanced by Mr G even taken collectively provided a basis for an adjournment of the proceedings.

I am satisfied that refusing an adjournment did not occasion procedural unfairness or constitute a significant miscarriage of justice. The father had ample opportunity to prepare his case, to adduce relevant expert evidence, and to challenge the applicant’s evidence through cross‑examination. The matters relied upon in support of adjournment did not raise any realistic prospect that the outcome of the proceedings would have been affected had the hearing been delayed, and the adjournment was refused.

Additional preliminary matters

After dealing with the adjournment application I turned to the outstanding Applications in a Proceeding. They were either dismissed or withdrawn for reasons provided on the day. I note that Mr G remained of the view that I was permitted to make parenting orders as part of these proceedings, notwithstanding that the Part VII parenting proceedings were stayed pending resolution of the Hague application. His client sought time with the children. Whilst reg 25 and 25A of the Regulations do permit the court “if the court is satisfied it is desirable to do so” to make an order regarding with whom a child is to spend time or communicate, that application is open to be made by the responsible Central Authority. No such application was agitated by the applicant.

I then heard objections to evidence. For reasons already provided I did not permit the father to rely on affidavits provided by Mr L or Ms M.

Whilst the father indicated he had objections to make to the applicant’s evidence, his attention was drawn to reg 29(2) of the Regulations which provides that any documents attached to an application or given in support of an application “is admissible as evidence of the facts stated in that application, request or document”. I advised the father that he could address me as to the weight to be given to the applicant’s documents in due course.

Immediately upon being advised the matter was proceeding, Mr G said he no longer held instructions and accordingly sought to withdraw. Leave was granted. I was not prepared to adjourn the matter for the father to prepare himself to act on his own behalf. He made his own determination to withdraw his instructions to his solicitor, the other parties were ready, and the matter needed to proceed.

In accordance with Full Court authority, I informed the father as to the manner in which the trial would proceed and the relevant procedures; Re F: Litigant in Person Guidelines [2001] FamCA348. He had a copy of the relevant Regulations, and all material relied upon by the other parties.

THE REGULATIONS

The Regulations are made pursuant to s 111B of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”). Those regulations are the domestic law that enables the performance of the obligations of Australia under the Convention. Those obligations are to ensure the return of children wrongfully removed or retained to their place of habitual residence upon satisfaction of jurisdictional facts (reg 16(1A)). The Court may exercise its discretion and not order the return of the children in the event the father is able to establish an exception to return (reg 16(3)).

Both Australia and Japan are signatories to the Convention, and it is in force between those two countries.

The Regulations require that applications for the return of children are dealt with expeditiously and as quickly as proper consideration of each matter permits (reg 15(2)). The hearing is generally summary in nature although cross examination may be appropriate in some matters.

ISSUES IN DISPUTE

The applicant asserts that the children have been wrongfully retained in Australia in accordance with reg 16(1) and 16(1A), since 4 September 2024, on the following basis:

(a) the application was made within one year of the child/ren’s retention in Australia;

(b) the children are under the age of 16;

(c) the children were habitually resident in Japan as at the date of 4 September 2024;

(d) the requesting parent, the mother, has rights of custody in relation to the children, which she was exercising immediately prior to the children’s retention; and

(e) the retention of the children was in breach of the mother’s rights of custody.

It is not in dispute that the application was made within one year of that date of asserted wrongful retention and that the children are under 16.

The father does not concede the children were habitually resident in Japan as at 4 September 2024. It is the father’s contention that the children’s habitual residence is and had always been Australia.

The father said he also disputed that the mother had rights of custody under Japanese law. Accordingly, he would concede neither that she was exercising those right, nor that those rights were breached when the children were not returned to Japan in September 2024.

In the event the jurisdictional facts are established, the father relies on the regulatory exceptions to return, namely:

(a) There is a grave risk that returning the children to Japan would expose them to physical or psychological harm or otherwise place them in an intolerable situation (reg 16(3)(b)); and

(b) The return of the children would not be permitted by the fundamental principles of Australia relating to the protection of human rights and fundamental freedoms (reg 16(3)(d)).

As the application was filed within one year of the children’s asserted retention, the question of whether the children are now settled is not relevant, save in the event a regulatory exception to return is established. Whether the children are settled in Australia would then be a consideration relevant to the exercise of my discretion.

Onus of proof

The requisite standard of proof, as required by s 140 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth), is the balance of probabilities.

The State Central Authority bears the onus of proving the jurisdictional facts.

The father bears the onus of proof to establish a regulatory exception to return.

Preliminary Matters

The hearing was conducted electronically via Webex.

An electronic hearing enabled the requesting mother to participate in the proceedings.

The evidence of the mother and the maternal grandmother was through an interpreter.

There was some delay in the matter proceeding to trial following the father raising constitutional matters in August 2025. He was given the opportunity to provide notice to the Attorney General pursuant to s 78B(2)(b) of the Judiciary Act 1903 (Cth), which he did not do.

When the matter returned to Court on 18 November 2025 the father advised he was no longer pressing constitutional matters. The matter was then set down for a three day hearing, with the father to file his material by 18 December 2025.

The father filed a further Application in a Proceeding on 10 December 2025. That matter was listed before me on 30 January 2026. That application was dismissed.

As at 30 January 2026 the father had still not filed his trial material. A new timetable for filing material was made that day. The father’s documents were due on 13 February 2026, the Independent Children's Lawyer was to be filed by 21 February 2026 and the applicant by 13 March 2026.

EVIDENCE AND DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON BY THE PARTIES

The State Central Authority relied upon the following documents:

(a) Form 2 Application filed 10 April 2025;

I have some reservations about the reliability of the father’s evidence where it bears upon matters central to the issues arising under the Convention. In several respects his account was framed by reference to legal characterisations – such as repeated assertions that there was “no shared intention” to reside in Japan and references to Australia as the children’s “habitual residence” – rather than by a chronological narrative of events. He experienced difficulty at times reconciling aspects of his earlier sworn material with the position he advanced at trial, explaining discrepancies by reference to affidavits having been prepared in different proceedings and requiring his statements to be read in context. In cross‑examination his answers at times did not directly engage with the question and instead returned to broader assertions that his actions were motivated by protection of the children. Overall, his evidence appeared to be shaped by the case theory he advanced – namely that there was no agreement for the children to live in Japan other than temporarily – rather than by a consistent account of the parties’ conduct as it unfolded.

JURISDICTIONAL FACTS

Habitual Residence (reg 16(1A)(b) of the Regulations)

Relevant legal principles

The relevant date to determine the children’s habitual residence is immediately before their retention in Australia (reg 16(1A)(b)) – being 4 September 2024.

The question of whether a person is habitually resident in a particular country is a question of fact. It requires the evaluation of all relevant circumstances.

The High Court considered the expression “habitual residence” in the case of LK v Director‑General, Department of Community Services [2009] HCA 9; (2009) 237 CLR 582 (“LK”). Their Honours said a wide range of facts and circumstances may be relevant including past and present intentions, the duration of a person’s connection with a place, and where the centre of a person’s personal and family life might be; “[t]he search must be for where a person resides and whether residence at that place can be described as habitual” [22]. However, their Honours said it was not possible to identify a closed set of criteria, or to identify any factors that should be given greater or lesser weight.

Their Honours said the habitual residence of those caring for the child would usually be an important consideration, as well as the intentions of both of the parents. However, intention is not to be given controlling weight [24], [28]. Their Honours also noted that an intention to abandon an old residence or to live in a place permanently or indefinitely is not required [38]. Rather the Court will consider whether the parents’ shared intention in living in a country has a sufficient degree of continuity to be properly described as settled.

In Department of Communities and Justice & Leoni [2020] FamCA 411, Harper J succinctly set out three important considerations identified by the High Court about the role of parental intention in determining habitual residence at [26] as follows:

a) First, individuals do not always act with a clearly formed and singular view of what it is intended (or hoped) that the future will hold. Their intentions may be ambiguous (at [29]);

b) Secondly, because a person's intentions may be ambiguous, in asking whether a person has abandoned residence in a place it is necessary to recognise the possibility that the person may not have formed a singular and irrevocable intention not to return, yet properly be described as no longer habitually resident in that place. Absence of a final decision positively rejecting the possibility of returning to a country in the foreseeable future is not necessarily inconsistent with ceasing to reside there habitually (at [33]).

c) Thirdly, when considering where a child is habitually resident, attention cannot be confined to the intentions of the parent who in fact has the day‑to‑day care of the child. It will usually be necessary to consider what each parent intends for the child. When parents are living together, young children will have the same habitual residence as their parents. No less importantly, it may be accepted that the general rule is that neither parent can unilaterally change that place of habitual residence. The assent of the other parent (or a Court order) would be necessary. But again, if it becomes necessary to examine the intentions of the parents, the possibility of ambiguity or uncertainty on the part of one or both of them must be acknowledged (at [34]).

In Zotkiewicz & Commissioner of Police (No.2) [2011] FamCAFC 147 (“Zotkiewicz”) the Full Court outlined at [82-83] that the Court should consider whether the parents had a shared intention that the child would live in a particular place, with a “sufficient degree of continuity that their purpose could be properly described as settled”. The Court must also consider whether the time spent in that place was “sufficiently appreciable for it to be said that the underlying reality of the connection between the child and [that place] was such as to justify a finding [of habitual residence]”.

Their Honour’s observed at [76]

There are, of course, cases where the acknowledged ambiguity or uncertainty in the intentions of one or both of the parents will become irrelevant because they have been pushed into the background by “the brute force of geography and duration”...Thus, in Zenel v Haddow 1993 SLT 975, a child was found to be habitually resident in Australia after 15 months, notwithstanding assertions there was no settled intention on the part of the parents to remain in Australia. In that case Lord Marnoch said (at 979):

It seems to me that, while intention is undoubtedly a very important consideration, there must come a stage when the objective facts point unequivocally to a person’s ordinary or habitual residence being in a particular place.

Additionally, their Honours said at [83] when considering habitual residence, the court is:

... obliged to construe the Regulations having regard to the principles and objects of the Convention, recognising “that the appropriate forum for resolving disputes relating to a child’s care, welfare and development is ordinarily the child’s country of habitual residence”.

Background

It is common ground that the mother is a citizen of Japan and the father a citizen of Australia. The parents met in City O in 2016 when the mother was travelling in Australia and thereafter commenced a de facto relationship.

The children were both born in Queensland and their births were registered there. X was born while the parents were residing in City F. The family later moved to Brisbane, where Y was born.

During the relationship the family relocated on a number of occasions, including between City F and various suburbs of Brisbane, to accommodate the father’s work commitments.

The mother subsequently registered the children’s births in Japan. The father is not recorded on the Japanese birth register. That position is consistent with Japanese law, which requires the consent of an unmarried father for his name to be recorded. Despite requests by the mother, including as recently as July 2025, the father has not provided that consent.

The children are dual citizens of Australia and Japan.

In November 2022 the mother travelled to Japan with the children for approximately six weeks. During that period X attended daycare in Japan.

In March 2023 the father entered into a 12‑month residential lease for a property in Brisbane.

Travel to Japan in April 2023

In about April 2023 the mother and the children travelled to Japan with the father’s encouragement and consent, where they were enrolled in kindergarten and daycare.

The mother said she considered the parents’ relationship at that time had ended and that the parents had discussed their separation. The mother’s evidence was that with the father’s encouragement, she and the children were to relocate to Japan and reside there on an ongoing basis. The mother said the father suggested the relocation because she had limited family supports in Australia, the family had a history of relocating to accommodate the father’s work and the father would not commit to remaining with one employer. Conversely, in Japan she and the children could live in a property owned jointly by her and the maternal grandmother, which would provide the children with stability and extended family support. She said the children would also be able to attend consistent schools and pre-schools in Japan rather than moving frequently as they had done in Australia. The mother said it was further agreed that the children would spend periods of the Japanese summer holidays in Australia, including in 2023 and 2024.

The maternal grandmother provided a statement confirming that the mother and children returned to Japan in April 2023. It was her understanding that the parents separated around that time.

The father asserted that the April 2023 travel to Japan was for the purpose of a holiday rather than relocation. He denied the parents were separated at this time.

The mother’s evidence was that, although the parents were separated, the father stayed in the home with her and the children when he was present in Japan in June and July 2023. She said this was done to maintain a sense of normality for the children and for cultural reasons, and it had been difficult to secure separate accommodation for him. Her evidence is the parents slept separately. She also acknowledged the father continued to make financial contributions towards the support of her and the children. She also set up her own bank account.

The mother denied that the parties continued their relationship after mid‑2023. She said this was confirmed in discussion with the father in June 2023 when he came to Japan for about seven days, and she confronted him about her belief he was seeing another woman. She said the parents discussed what they should tell the children about their separation. The mother accepted there were ongoing communications in relation to the children and practical matters, but said those communications were as co‑parents rather than partners.

The father admitted the mother accused him of seeing another woman, which he denied. He maintained the parents continued their relationship. However, it is also apparent from an affidavit filed by the father on 7 July 2025 that he obtained legal advice in July 2023 which referred, among other things, to his intention to end the relationship. That provides some corroboration of the mother’s narrative.

The mother applied for single‑parent payments in Japan shortly thereafter. The father said that application was made without his knowledge.

The mother also said she went with X to tour a primary school in Japan, and bought the school backpack. She said over time the parents spoke about securing accommodation in the catchment area for that school and had many detailed discussions about living in Japan. The maternal grandmother corroborated hearing the parents’ discussions about X attending primary school in Japan.

Travel to Australia July 2023

In July 2023 the parents and children travelled to Australia together. The father characterised that travel as the family “returning home”. The mother said it was a one‑month holiday and that the children remained enrolled in daycare in Japan, consistent with the agreement that she and the children would return there.

X attended kindergarten in Australia for about 10 days during that trip.

The father deposed that whilst in Australia both parents toured a Steiner school and signed an enrolment form for X to commence there in 2025. The mother denied enrolling X in any school for 2025. The enrolment form exhibited by the father does not contain the mother’s signature.

Arrangements from August 2023

In late August 2023 the family travelled to Japan again. They returned to the same accommodation in City P. The children resumed at their kindergarten and day care attendance. They were also engaged in swimming, soccer and English lessons.

At that time the father was employed as a FIFO worker. He worked a four‑week roster, spending approximately two weeks working in Australia and two weeks in Japan with the family. He continued that pattern of travel until July 2024. The father admitted he told his employer he was moving to Japan. He said he told them that was for a year, but he did not adduce any evidence from his employer to that effect.

In an affidavit sworn on 29 August 2024 in parenting proceedings, the father acknowledged that the parents had discussed the children’s living arrangements and had agreed there would be benefit in the children experiencing life in Japan and potentially elsewhere. He deposed that the family “relocated” to City P in or about August 2023 and intended to live in Japan for a period of up to one year, and then “either return to Australia or consider a year in Europe”. In his oral evidence the father acknowledged he contemplated living somewhere in Europe or moving to Country Q. Whilst he had used the word “relocate” in the affidavit, in his oral evidence the father said he meant that they went “for a temporary agreed period of time”. He also acknowledged the intention was for the children to remain in Japan until the parents decided to return to Australia, or live elsewhere, but added there was never a mutual agreement the children would permanently live outside Australia.

The mother denied the parents had conversations about how long they would remain in Japan or when she and children would return to Australia. She agreed the father suggested the family might move to Country Q or Europe but she said she had no intention to live elsewhere.

In text communications in about August 2023, the father’s sister expressed an understanding that the family was moving to Japan.

The father now asserts that the family’s time in Japan between August 2023 and August 2024 was a temporary holiday.

Events in 2024

In March 2024 the mother commenced employment in Japan as a fitness professional.

In May 2024 the mother told a friend in Australia that she and the children would be travelling to Australia in August for approximately one month. In that communication she enquired about the possibility of X attending school on a temporary basis during that visit.

As a result of those enquiries, the parents made contact with R School and an enrolment application was completed in June 2024.

In August 2024 the mother and father inspected a unit in City P together that was available to lease from September 2024. It is the mother’s evidence they inspected the premises with a view to the mother leasing the property when she returned from Australia, as it was in the catchment area for the primary school the parents had agreed X would commence attending.

The mother and children remained in Japan until early August 2024, when they travelled to Australia with return flights booked for 4 September 2024. The mother said she was scheduled to return to work in Japan in early September 2024.

On arrival in Australia the mother and children stayed briefly with the paternal grandmother in City F before there was an altercation between the parents. The children did not attend daycare or school during that period.

The mother said she ultimately did not pursue enrolment for X at R School, taking the view that it was impractical for a stay of only a few weeks.

In his affidavit of 29 August 2024, the father described this travel to Australia as a “holiday”. However, in these proceedings the father maintains the children were returning home to Australia and his retention of the children’s passports was to protect them from being removed from Australia without his consent.

Events in Australia in 2024

It is common ground that in early August 2024 there was an argument between the parents in City F. The mother and children remained in the same accommodation as the father until late August 2024, when they were assisted by police and family violence support services to relocate. The father retained the children’s Australian and Japanese passports.

The father’s evidence was that the parents separated only in August 2024. As already observed, according to an affidavit the father prepared and filed himself on 7 July 2025 he had obtained advice from a family lawyer in July 2023, who purportedly told him to “get my sons back to birth country as was originally planned around the 1 year mark, end the relationship as I intended to and retain possession of our sons passports to ensure their safety” (errors in the original).

It is clear the mother expected to return to Japan with the children on 4 September 2024 however the father’s actions prevented her from doing so.

After sustained questioning the father ultimately said it was his intention to retain the children in Australia once they arrived in August 2024, notwithstanding that the parents had booked return tickets. He maintained this was necessary to “protect the children”.

Discussion

The applicant contends that, whatever uncertainty may have existed prior to August 2023 as to the parents’ intentions, by that date and thereafter the objective facts point decisively to Japan as the children’s habitual residence at the relevant time, being immediately before their retention in Australia on 4 September 2024.

The father contends that the parents never formed a joint intention for the children to leave Australia on anything other than a temporary basis, and that Australia accordingly remained the children’s habitual residence throughout.

Resolution of that dispute requires a broad factual inquiry directed not only to parental intention, but to the underlying reality of the children’s lives at the relevant time.

As at April 2023, X was four years old and Y was a toddler. Between April 2023 and 4 September 2024, the children lived in Japan for approximately fifteen months. During that period they resided in stable accommodation, attended the same kindergarten and daycare in City P, participated in extracurricular activities, spent substantial time with members of the maternal family, and spoke Japanese fluently. Japan was the place in which their day‑to‑day lives were conducted.

The evidence does not support the existence of any shared agreement as to when, or even whether, the children would return to Australia to live. Even on the father’s account, the children’s time in Japan was not referable to a fixed or clearly defined return date. Rather, the arrangement contemplated that the children would live in Japan for an indeterminate period – whether one year, two years, or longer – with any future move to Australia or elsewhere to be decided later.

That uncertainty is reflected in the parents’ communications. At various points, the father referred to returning to Australia, remaining longer in Japan, or potentially relocating elsewhere. There was inconsistency between the father’s assertion in these proceedings that the agreement was for one year and references he made in communications to the intention being to be in Japan for 18 months or two years. The mother expressed a preference to continue living in Japan. Taken as a whole, the communications do not evidence a shared or settled plan for the children’s return to Australia, but rather an open‑ended arrangement accompanied by ongoing uncertainty and divergence of understanding.

Some of the father’s conduct is consistent with a relocation. In mid‑2023 the father terminated the lease and vacated the accommodation in Brisbane, sold his car, and told his employer that he was moving to Japan (albeit, on his account, for a defined period). His sister appeared to understand the family to be moving to Japan. The father thereafter worked on a FIFO basis, spending alternate periods in Australia and Japan until July 2024. That pattern continued without any concrete steps being taken to re‑establish the children’s lives in Australia.

The father places emphasis on discussions and documentation concerning Australian schooling as supporting a finding that the parents shared an intention for the children to return to Australia to live. I do not agree.

The evidence establishes that temporary enrolments during visits were a recurring feature of the family’s arrangements. The children had previously attended childcare or kindergarten on a short‑term basis when travelling, including X’s attendance at day care in Japan during a visit in late 2022 and at kindergarten in Australia for approximately 10 days during the one‑month stay in July 2023. Given that history, the parents’ enquiries regarding schools or kindergartens in Australia, including visiting potential schools, do not point unequivocally to an intention to return to Australia or for the children to commence permanent schooling here.

I am not satisfied the documents from the Australian schools relied upon by the father demonstrate a shared and settled parental intention that the children would return to Australia to live. The Steiner school enrolment form relied upon by the father was not signed by the mother. The R School enrolment material was generated in the context of a proposed short stay in Australia and was proximity‑based to the temporary accommodation booked by the mother. The mother’s communication with her friend in May 2024 regarding the possibility of a school enrolment whilst the children were in Australia in August provides further corroboration that the intention was for temporary attendance. Whilst the enrolment form with the father’s handwritten amendment “kinder-2024” and “prep-2025” was signed by both parents, the father did not contradict the mother’s evidence that she did not read the document and was only presented with the page for her to sign.

It is not in dispute that the children remaining enrolled in their Japanese educational settings during periods of travel to Australia in 2023 and 2024.

Viewed as a whole, the schooling‑related text message communications and documents are explicable by reference to temporary arrangements made in anticipation of visits, and do not unequivocally evidence a shared, settled intention that the children would be returning permanently to Australia.

To the extent there were misunderstandings between the parents as to the significance of particular documents or communications, those arose in the context of language barriers and imperfect translation. To that end, I accept the mother’s evidence that language difficulties, cultural differences and the use of translation apps sometimes resulted in inaccuracies that did not reflect her intended meaning. She further said that she did not always correct the father’s assertions in text messages due to language difficulties, time pressures associated with caring for the children, and a desire to avoid conflict.

There were, however, multiple objective indicators pointing towards Japan as the anticipated place of ongoing residence. Those included the mother’s employment in Japan; the establishment of education‑related financial arrangements directed to schooling in Japan; inspection by both parents of prospective long‑term accommodation in City P in the school catchment area discussed by the parents; advice given to the children’s kindergarten and daycare that the August 2024 travel to Australia was temporary; and confirmation from those institutions that the children were expected to return in early September 2024 and to continue there in 2025. The mother’s employer likewise expected her return following that travel. These matters are consistent only with Japan continuing to be the centre of the children’s lives at the relevant time.

It is also significant that return flights from Australia to Japan were booked for 4 September 2024, and that the father himself had further travel planned between the two countries later that month. Those arrangements are consistent with a shared understanding that the children would be returning to Japan and are inconsistent with the father’s contention that the agreement was that the children would return to Australia permanently after having spent a year in Japan.

It is plain that the children would have returned to Japan on 4 September 2024 but for the father’s unilateral intervention.

Determination

In determining a child’s habitual residence, the absence of a singular, final or irrevocable parental intention as to future residence is not determinative. Parental intentions may be conditional, unresolved or ambiguous, and habitual residence does not depend upon proof of a joint, settled intention to permanently abandon one country or settle indefinitely in another. The inquiry is a broad factual one, including a consideration of the reality of the child’s connection with a place at the relevant time. That invites consideration of a wide range of factors including settled purpose, actual and intended duration of stay, integration and assimilation into day to day life and the strength of ties to the state and any other state.

A child may cease to be habitually resident in a country notwithstanding that a parent has not excluded the possibility of return, and notwithstanding that a new habitual residence has not yet been acquired. Conversely, habitual residence in a new country may be established even where parental intention remains uncertain, if the objective circumstances demonstrate genuine integration, continuity, and a real and active connection with that place.

As observed by the Full Court in Zotkiewicz at [76], where parental intentions are ambiguous or contested, the passage of time may eventually push intention “into the background” if the objective facts point unequivocally to the children’s habitual residence being in a particular place.

As to parental intention, I am satisfied the mother understood there was an agreement for her and the children to relocate to Japan in 2023, and for the children to return to Australia for one month each year and she acted on that agreement.

I am also satisfied that, by at least August 2023, the father intended that the children live in Japan for an appreciable but undefined period, with decisions about any later return to Australia or move elsewhere to be deferred. There was no concluded agreement as to when the children would leave Japan. In those circumstances, I do not accept the father’s characterisation of the mother as acting unilaterally.

From August 2023 until July 2024, the father worked in Australia and returned to Japan regularly, participating in family life there and engaging in decisions and conduct consistent with the children’s ongoing residence in Japan including inspecting a unit, agreeing on a school for X and purchasing return flights. His actions and communications were such as to convey agreement to, or at least acquiescence in, the children continuing to live there for the foreseeable future.

As already observed, parental intention is not determinative; habitual residence in a new place can be found even where parental intention is ambiguous, unresolved or contested, where such finding is supported by the objective circumstances of the children’s reality and connection with that place.

This is not a case of involuntary or constrained residence of the kind considered in Zotkiewicz. There is no controversy that the children’s residence in Japan between April 2023 and August 2024 was voluntary, consensual, and planned by both parents.

I am satisfied that as at immediately before 4 September 2024 the children spent the vast majority of the preceding 17 months in Japan and were settled there. During that appreciable period they lived in stable accommodation, attended a consistent kindergarten and childcare, participated in daily life and extracurricular activities, maintained family and social connections and conducted their lives primarily in Japan. Those objective facts demonstrate a significant degree of continuity and integration.

These circumstances point unequivocally to Japan being the place in which the children resided habitually at the relevant time and being the centre of their lives.

The trip to Australia in August 2024 was intended, at least until shortly before its conclusion, to be temporary, with a return to Japan planned for 4 September 2024. The father accepted that his retention of the children’s passports was motivated by a desire to prevent that return. That conduct does not alter the children’s habitual residence immediately prior to the retention.

For these reasons I find the children were habitually resident in Japan immediately before their retention in Australia on 4 September 2024.

Rights of custody

Whilst the father would not concede that the mother had rights of custody under Japanese law immediately prior to the children’s retention in Australia, that the retention was in breach of those rights, and the mother was exercising those rights (reg 16(1A)(c)-(e)), those factors are readily made out.

It is not in dispute that the parents were not married, and that the father had not acknowledged paternity by way of registration in Japan.

The uncontradicted evidence of Dr Nakamura is that in those circumstances, under Japanese law the mother had sole parental responsibility for the children. They were living with her in Japan, and but for the father preventing the children from leaving Australia the mother would have continued to exercise her rights under Japanese law. As already observed the father had many months to obtain his own expert evidence if he wished to contradict that given by Dr Nakamura.

I am satisfied the mother had rights of custody under Japanese law, that she was exercising those rights and that the father’s actions which have retained the children in Australia were in breach of those rights of custody.

HAVE THE JURISDICTIONAL FACTS BEEN SATISFIED

I am satisfied that all the requirements of reg 16(1A) of the Regulations have been satisfied.

Accordingly, I find the children’s retention which occurred on 4 September 2024 was wrongful.

REGULATORY EXCEPTIONS TO RETURN

As already observed, the Regulations place the onus on the father to establish, to the requisite standard, the existence of a regulatory exception to return.

Grave risk of physical or psychological harm or placing the child in an intolerable situation (reg 16(3)(b))

The father is not required to establish that harm will occur if the children are returned to Japan. He must, however, satisfy me that there is a grave risk that the children would be exposed to physical or psychological harm, or placed in an intolerable situation, as a consequence of their return. This requires a forward‑looking assessment of the situation the children would face on return and whether the magnitude of the risk warrants the description “grave” (DP v Commonwealth Central Authority (2001) 206 CLR 401) (“DP”).

That inquiry inevitably involves some consideration of the interests of the children, but it is not a substitute for a best‑interests assessment, which is reserved to the court of habitual residence (DP).

As the majority in DP observed, the exception is not engaged by the ordinary distress, anxiety or disruption inherent in being returned to another country pursuant to the Convention. Rather, it directs attention to risks beyond those predictable consequences, and will ordinarily require clear and compelling evidence. The exception is not to be read broadly or narrowly, but given the meaning its words require.

The Court may also consider whether any identified risk can be sufficiently mitigated by the existence of protective measures or the capacity of the authorities in the receiving state to make appropriate arrangements for the children’s welfare (Gsponer v Director‑General of Community Services (Vic) (1989) FLC 92‑001 (“Gsponer”); De L v Director‑General, NSW Department of Community Services & Anor (1996) 187 CLR 640) (“De L”).

Further, in assessing the gravity of the risk of harm to which the children will be exposed, I must take into account the ability of the Japanese authorities to make suitable arrangements for the care of the children. The Full Court said in Gsponer at 77,160:

There is no reason why this court should not assume that once the child is so returned, the courts in that country are not appropriately equipped to make suitable arrangements for the child’s welfare. Indeed the entry by Australia into this convention with the other countries may justify the assumption that the Australian government is satisfied to that effect.

Similarly, the High Court of Australia said in De L at 658:

The Regulations reflect the objects of the Convention to settle issues of jurisdiction between the Contracting States by favouring the forum which has been the habitual residence of the child. The underlying premise is that, once the forum is located in this way, each Contracting State has faith in the domestic law of the other contracting states to deal in a proper fashion with matters relating to the custody of children under the age of 16.

In State Central Authority & Del Rosario [2019] FamCA 607 at [19(b)], Bennett J said:

It is settled that the assessment of grave risk of harm must be exposure to harm as a consequence of return and not exposure to harm which might emerge some later time if, after return, an unsatisfactory situation is allowed to persist without alteration, per Lord Prosser in McCarthy v McCarthy (1994) SLT 743, 747.

Discussion

The father asserts that a return order would expose the children to a grave risk because:

(a) they will be severed from Country, kinship and Aboriginal culture;

(b) they will be deprived of a meaningful relationship with him;

(c) the mother has perpetrated abuse; and

(d) taken together, these matters would place the children in an intolerable situation.

He contends these risks cannot be mitigated by any conditions of return and can only be avoided by the children remaining in Australia.

Cultural connection

The father emphasised that the children are Aboriginal and entitled to cultural connection, asserting that meaningful transmission of culture must be face‑to‑face and on Country. He said time in Australia intermittently would not be sufficient to meet his cultural obligations to educate the children and that the mother cannot adequately facilitate this in Japan.

I accept as a general proposition that severance of Aboriginal children from Country and kinship systems has the potential to cause significant harm. However, my task is to assess whether these children face a grave risk of physical or psychological harm, or would otherwise be placed in an intolerable situation, if they are returned to Japan in the circumstances of this case.

The evidentiary foundation for the asserted risk is limited. The father described visits to various places of cultural significance reflecting efforts by him to develop the children’s connection to Country. The mother’s evidence – which was not the subject of challenge by the father and which I accept – was that the children had not been meaningfully introduced to Aboriginal language, Elders, ceremonies, or structured cultural practices. She also said she was not aware that trips to places such as Region S were intended by the father as cultural connection for the children, and she understood them to be recreational holidays. She said the father in no way indicated that visits to beaches or other places in Region S reflected any efforts by the father to build the children’s connection to their traditional lands. Additionally, the parents did not previously discuss Aboriginal language acquisition, and instead discussed the children learning other languages.

The children lived in Japan for much of 2023 and 2024 with the father’s agreement. The father conceded at that time the parents prioritised the children’s immersion in Japanese culture. There is no evidence that the children’s absence from Australia during that period caused them psychological harm or placed them in an intolerable situation. Further, the father himself contemplated the children living in other jurisdictions, including Europe and Country Q, which sits uncomfortably with the assertion that residence outside Australia necessarily poses a grave cultural risk.

The father sought to rely on affidavit material from an elder of the D people. That material went beyond permissible opinion evidence and into matters of psychological impact and historical policy consequence, without expertise or factual connection to these children. It was therefore excluded.

The children are dual citizens of Australia and Japan and have cultural heritage from both parents.

Dr Nakamura’s evidence was that the children’s Aboriginal heritage is a matter that can be taken into account by the Japanese family court in determining appropriate arrangements for the children.

On the evidence before me, I am not satisfied that return to Japan would expose these children to a grave risk of psychological harm arising from loss of cultural connection. This finding does not reflect any diminution of the importance of Aboriginal cultural identity generally, but rather the absence of evidence that return to Japan would expose these children to harm as contemplated by reg 16(3)(b).

Deprivation of a relationship with the father

The father asserts that the operation of Japanese family law is such that he will be effectively excluded from the children’s lives if they are returned. He sought to rely on an anecdotal account from a friend which I excluded, and no expert evidence was adduced in support of that contention.

I accept the expert evidence of Dr Nakamura. She explained the current and newly amended Japanese legal framework, including avenues by which paternity can be recognised and applications can be made for parental authority and visitation. She acknowledged there are limitations in the enforcement of orders and agreements (referring to the imposition of financial penalties for continued non-compliance, but no court processes to impose criminal or quasi criminal sanctions), but did not support the proposition that the Japanese system will necessarily, or even probably, result in the father being excluded from the children’s lives until adulthood.

Dr Nakamura gave evidence that amendments to Japanese family law effective from 1 April 2026 introduce the possibility of shared parental responsibility. She confirmed the father can apply for visitation and contact orders in Japan, and if he acknowledges paternity under the Japanese registration system, he will be eligible to apply for joint parental authority.

Dr Nakamura said the court’s determination is based on the best interests of the child, with the court taking into account matters such as parental relationships, communication, and any history of family violence or abuse. She said the court could also consider the Indigenous heritage of the parents and children.

The mother deposed she would be agreeable to entering into consent orders with the Country P Family Court that she facilitate contact between the father and the children, including during his visits to Japan and through holiday time in Australia. I do not have evidence about whether such an agreement could be drafted in Australia, and then registered or enforced in Japan.

While the father expresses scepticism, particularly given that the mother has not facilitated any physical time between the father and the children since August 2024, speculation about future non‑compliance cannot, without more, establish a grave risk. This is particularly so where I have no evidence as to why time has not been facilitated, and the father did not explore this at trial.

While the application of the new reforms remains to be fully tested, I cannot be satisfied that the Japanese courts would be unable or unwilling to engage with the father’s applications.

I also note the authorities make it plain that this Court will not easily assume that courts in another Convention country – whose entry into the Convention has been accepted by Australia – cannot deal in a proper fashion with matters relating to the care of children; see De L at 658; Gsponer 77,160.

In the absence of cogent expert evidence demonstrating that return will expose the children to a grave risk of losing their relationship with the father as a consequence of the Japanese legal system, this aspect of the exception is not established.

Allegations of abuse

The father did not advance detailed allegations of violence or impaired parenting capacity in his trial affidavit. He said that was an oversight, and he had deposed to such matters in other affidavits.

At any rate, there is no corroborative evidence from Child Protection, from the children’s school or kindergarten or from any person engaged in the care of the children that raises concerns regarding the mother’s care of the children. There is also no evidence adduced from any medical or allied health professional to support a finding that the mother suffers from poor mental health such that her parenting capacity is impaired to the extent the children are at risk in her care.

The father’s own conduct is inconsistent with a genuine belief that the children are at risk in the mother’s care. The mother was the children’s primary carer when he was working, he consented to the children living with the mother in Japan for extended periods, including during his FIFO work arrangements, and previously proposed parenting orders that involved the children remaining primarily in the mother’s care.

These matters strongly undermine the contention that a return to Japan would expose the children to a grave risk of harm arising from the mother’s conduct or mental health. This aspect of the exception is also not established.

Intolerable situation

The concept of an “intolerable situation” requires circumstances that are objectively unbearable for the child, not merely undesirable or less preferable from the perspective of one parent.

The father relies on the combined effect of alleged cultural severance and loss of relationship with him. For the reasons already given, none of these matters are made out to the requisite standard. There is no evidence that the children experienced harm during their prior extended residence in Japan, nor that the courts of Japan cannot fashion arrangements addressing their welfare. This exception is also not established.

Conclusion as to grave risk and intolerable situation

Considered individually and cumulatively, the matters advanced by the father do not establish that return to Japan would expose the children to a grave risk of physical or psychological harm, or place them in an intolerable situation within the meaning of reg 16(3)(b). The exception is therefore not made out.

The children’s return would not be permitted by the fundamental principles of Australia relating to the protection of human rights and fundamental freedoms reg 16(3)(d)

The father contends that returning the children to Japan would contravene Australia’s fundamental principles relating to the protection of human rights and fundamental freedoms. He asserts that return would impermissibly interfere with the children’s cultural identity as Aboriginal children and would effectively sever their relationship with him due to the operation of Japanese family law.

Regulation 16(3)(d) reflects Article 20 of the Convention. It is an exception of rare application. The Full Court has described it as extremely narrow, confined to circumstances in which return would be prohibited by Australia’s fundamental human rights principles, and not merely incompatible with them (Director‑General, Department of Families, Youth and Community Care & Bennett [2000] FamCA 253 (“Bennett”)).

As the Full Court explained in Bennett at [56], Article 20 was intended to apply only on rare occasions where return would “utterly shock the conscience of the court or offend all notions of due process”. It is not enough to demonstrate hardship, disadvantage, or even serious concern. The father must establish that return is prohibited by Australia’s fundamental human rights principles (McCall & McCall State Central Authority (Applicant); Attorney-General of the Commonwealth (Intervener) (1995) FLC 92‑551). It is a high threshold.

This Court is not permitted to assume either that Australia affords superior protection of human rights compared with other Convention countries, or that foreign courts cannot adequately deal with matters such as Indigenous heritage, cultural identity or parental relationships. To do so would be contrary to the mutual trust that underpins the Convention (Bennett).

The father’s case under reg 16(3)(d) substantially overlaps with the matters relied upon under reg 16(3)(b), namely:

(a) alleged cultural severance arising from the children living in Japan; and

(b) alleged exclusion of the father from the children’s lives due to Japanese family law.

For the reasons already given in relation to the grave risk exception, those matters do not establish that return to Japan would expose the children to serious cultural harm, nor that the Japanese legal system is incapable of addressing parental relationships or cultural considerations. Those findings are equally relevant here.

It is uncontroversial that the children are of Aboriginal descent. However, the evidence does not establish that Aboriginal cultural practices formed a sustained or structured part of the children’s lived experiences prior to 2023. The children subsequently lived outside Australia for much of 2023 and 2024 with the father’s agreement. There is no evidence that during that period their absence from Australia infringed their human rights or resulted in cultural harm, let alone harm of a kind that would render return impermissible.

The father himself does not reside consistently on Country and has worked for extended periods outside Australia. He also previously contemplated the children residing in other overseas jurisdictions. These matters are difficult to reconcile with a contention that residence outside Australia is inherently incompatible with the children’s fundamental human rights.

The mother acknowledges the importance of the children understanding their Aboriginal heritage and has deposed to her willingness to facilitate their connection to that aspect of their identity. There is no basis to conclude that return to Japan would prevent such consideration, or that Japanese courts are unable to take cultural heritage into account in determining children’s arrangements, as confirmed by the expert evidence of Dr Nakamura.

Nor am I satisfied that the asserted risk of the father being excluded from the children’s lives engages reg 16(3)(d). As explained earlier, there is a legal pathway available to the father in Japan to pursue a relationship with the children. The father’s concerns in this regard relate to anticipated dissatisfaction with a foreign legal system, not to a denial of fundamental human rights or procedural fairness.

The circumstances of the children’s return to Japan – where they have lived previously, with their Japanese mother, in accordance with Australia’s obligations under the Convention – do not approach the extreme threshold required to engage reg 16(3)(d). Return would not shock the conscience of the court, offend all notions of due process, or be prohibited by Australia’s fundamental principles relating to the protection of human rights and fundamental freedoms.

Accordingly, the exception in reg 16(3)(d) is not made out.

ORDER TO RETURN

Having determined;

(a) the applicant has established all the jurisdictional facts, including that the children were habitually resident in Japan immediately before their wrongful retention in Australia on 4 September 2024;

(b) the application was filed within one year of that wrongful retention; and

(c) that none of the regulatory exceptions to return has been established;

the Court is obliged to make an order for the children’s return pursuant to reg 16(1) of the Regulations.

The making of a return order does not constitute a determination of the children’s best interests in a general sense. Rather, it reflects the Convention’s premise that questions concerning the children’s welfare, care and future arrangements are to be determined by the courts of Japan as the forum of habitual residence. By making orders for the children’s return, I am giving effect to Australia’s obligations under the Convention by restoring the jurisdiction of the courts of the country of habitual residence.

As to the orders I am making, I am satisfied they are proportionate and appropriate to secure the children’s return in a manner consistent with the Convention and the Regulations, and that ancillary orders relating to travel documentation and interim restraints are necessary to ensure the effective implementation of the return.

In circumstances where the father has retained the children’s passports and has indicated they are no longer in his possession, it is both necessary and appropriate to authorise the mother solely to obtain replacement travel documents so that the return can occur without further delay.

Orders will therefore be made requiring the children’s prompt return to Japan forthwith upon travel documents being procured, together with ancillary and protective orders to facilitate that return and preserve the status quo pending departure.

I will make orders accordingly.
